Rooting (Android) and Jailbreaking (iOS) achieve the same goal: gaining elevated (root/admin) privileges and bypassing manufacturer restrictions. This disables critical OS security controls like application sandboxing and mandatory code signing.

When an enterprise Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution is bypassed via jailbreaking (using tools like unc0ver), corporate data is exposed to unauthorized access and malware, presenting a severe data exfiltration risk that requires immediate incident response.

topics.title: OWASP Mobile Top 10 & App Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
The OWASP Mobile Top 10 highlights critical mobile risks:

  • M1: Improper Credential Usage: The #1 risk, encompassing hardcoded credentials, insecure API keys, and insecure credential transmission.
  • Insecure Data Storage: For example, Android SharedPreferences stored as unencrypted XML files in /data/data/<package_name>/shared_prefs/ can be read by any attacker with root access.
  • Lack of Certificate Pinning: Without certificate pinning, an app trusts any certificate signed by a trusted CA. This allows attackers who install a rogue CA certificate on a device to perform Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks on HTTPS traffic.

topics.title: Mobile Attack Vectors & Dynamic Instrumentation
topics.content:
Mobile malware often disguises itself as benign apps (e.g., a flashlight app) but requests excessive and unnecessary permissions like READ_SMS, CAMERA, and ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, functioning as Spyware or a Trojan.

Advanced malware evades static analysis using Dynamic Code Loading. By using DexClassLoader, an app can load a secondary malicious DEX file from a remote server at runtime, bypassing app store scanners.

Security researchers use tools like Frida for dynamic instrumentation. It injects JavaScript into running processes, allowing runtime modification, method hooking, and bypassing security controls like root detection and SSL pinning.

topics.title: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
topics.content:
Attackers use various techniques to bypass network defenses:

  • Decoy Scanning: Using Nmap's -D flag sends spoofed source IPs alongside the attacker's real IP to confuse Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS).
  • Firewalking: Manipulating IP TTL values in a traceroute-like fashion to map out firewall ACLs and discover open ports.
  • Packet Fragmentation: Splitting payloads into smaller fragments to evade IDS signature matching, hoping the IDS fails to reassemble them correctly.
  • Source Routing: Allowing the sender to specify the exact route a packet takes, potentially bypassing security devices.
  • Obfuscation: Masking payloads using URL encoding (e.g., %2e%2e%2f) or hex encoding to evade string signatures.
  • TCP Chaffing (Insertion): Sending garbage packets with fake TTLs or bad checksums that the IDS processes but the target host drops.
  • Slow Port Scans: Evading time-based IDS thresholds by sending packets very slowly (e.g., one every 5 minutes).
  • Snort Rules: Consist of a Rule Header (action, protocol, IPs, ports) and Rule Options (signatures and alerts).
  • Honeypots: Low-interaction honeypots simulate services, while high-interaction honeypots run actual vulnerable operating systems for detailed behavioral analysis.
  • Screened Subnet: A firewall architecture utilizing two firewalls to create a DMZ separating the internal network from the internet.

modules.title: IoT and OT Hacking
modules.description: Covers Internet of Things architecture and protocols, OT/SCADA security concepts, IoT attack surfaces, and core Cloud Computing security principles.
objectives: Understand IoT architecture, protocols, and communication standards
Identify IoT attack surfaces and common vulnerabilities
Explain OT/SCADA concepts and attack vectors
Understand Cloud computing service models and shared responsibility
Identify Cloud-specific threats and serverless security risks

topics.title: IoT Protocols and Architecture
topics.content:
IoT uses lightweight protocols for constrained environments:

  • MQTT: A lightweight publish-subscribe messaging protocol. If a broker lacks proper ACLs, subscribing to the wildcard topic "#" allows an attacker to receive all messages, exposing sensor data, commands, and credentials.
  • CoAP: A lightweight UDP-based RESTful protocol using less overhead than HTTP, ideal for constrained devices.
  • Zigbee: A low-power, short-range wireless mesh networking protocol (IEEE 802.15.4) commonly used in smart homes.

topics.title: IoT Attack Surface and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
A major vulnerability in IoT is the use of default or hardcoded credentials (e.g., admin/admin), which the Mirai botnet heavily exploited to compromise millions of devices.

Other issues include unencrypted HTTP communications and a lack of digital signature verification for firmware updates, which allow Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks and malicious firmware injection. Furthermore, manufacturers sometimes embed the same private RSA key across all devices of a single model; extracting it from one device compromises the entire product line.

topics.title: OT/SCADA Security and Protocols
topics.content:
SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems monitor and control industrial processes. They are high-value targets because compromising them can cause severe physical damage. A PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) directly reads sensors and operates machinery.

OT protocols often lack modern security. Modbus TCP operates without authentication or encryption, meaning any device on the network can read and write PLC registers. To protect OT environments, organizations use the Purdue Model, a hierarchical network segmentation framework that isolates critical OT zones from IT networks.

topics.title: Cloud Computing Concepts
topics.content:
Cloud computing relies on the Shared Responsibility Model. In SaaS, while the provider manages the infrastructure, the customer remains responsible for securing application data and user access. A Private Cloud is dedicated solely to a single organization.

  • PaaS (Platform as a Service): Provides developers a framework to build applications without managing the underlying OS or hardware.
  • Container Escape: A critical threat where an attacker exploits vulnerabilities in a Docker container to break out and access the underlying host OS.
  • Storage Misconfigurations: Leaving cloud storage buckets (like AWS S3) publicly readable is a leading cause of data breaches.
  • Cryptojacking: Illicitly utilizing stolen cloud compute resources to mine cryptocurrency.
  • SSRF to Metadata Service: Exploiting SSRF in a cloud VM to query 169.254.169.254 and steal temporary IAM credentials.
  • Serverless (FaaS): The provider secures the infrastructure, but the primary risk shifts to insecure application code (e.g., injection flaws).
  • CASB: A Cloud Access Security Broker acts as a gatekeeper enforcing security and compliance policies between consumers and providers.

modules.title: Cloud Computing and Applied Cryptography
modules.description: Covers advanced cloud security, zero trust, IAM risks, container vulnerabilities, and fundamental cryptography concepts including symmetric/asymmetric algorithms, hashing, and cryptanalysis.
objectives: Explain the shared responsibility model in IaaS
Assess container and serverless security configurations
Implement Zero Trust architecture and CSPM tools
Understand symmetric and asymmetric encryption principles
Identify cryptographic attacks and vulnerabilities

topics.title: Cloud Security and IAM Misconfigurations
topics.content:
In an IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) deployment, the customer is fully responsible for patching the guest operating system, securing applications, and protecting data.

Misconfigured IAM policies, such as {"Effect": "Allow", "Action": "*", "Resource": "*"}, grant full administrative access, leading to total account compromise if credentials are leaked. AWS S3 buckets with public-read ACLs allow anyone on the internet to list and download all contents without authentication. To prevent SSRF attacks from stealing metadata credentials, AWS introduced IMDSv2, requiring a session token.

topics.title: Container Security, Serverless, and Zero Trust
topics.content:
Running Docker containers as root is dangerous; a container escape gives the attacker root access on the host. In Kubernetes, running pods with hostNetwork: true and privileged: true completely breaks container isolation, providing direct access to host network interfaces and kernel capabilities.

Serverless computing (FaaS) introduces risks like over-privileged functions, insecure dependencies, and injection via event data.

The Zero Trust model assumes no entity is inherently trusted, requiring continuous verification regardless of network location. CSPM (Cloud Security Posture Management) tools help maintain this by continuously monitoring infrastructure for misconfigurations and compliance violations.

topics.title: Cryptography Fundamentals
topics.content:
Symmetric encryption uses the same shared key for encryption and decryption (e.g., AES, DES). Asymmetric encryption uses a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption, solving the key distribution problem (e.g., RSA, ECC).

  • AES: Advanced Encryption Standard uses a fixed block size of 128 bits.
  • DES & 3DES: DES has an effective key size of 56 bits. 3DES applies the DES cipher three times consecutively with up to three different keys.
  • RC4: A classic software stream cipher that encrypts bit-by-bit.
  • RSA: Security relies on the mathematical difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers.
  • ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography): Requires much shorter key lengths than RSA to provide the same level of security.
  • Diffie-Hellman: Protocol for securely exchanging keys over an insecure channel.

topics.title: Hashing, PKI, and Advanced Cryptography
topics.content:
A cryptographic hash function (like SHA-256) ensures data integrity by generating a fixed-size digest. Collision resistance ensures two different inputs don't produce the same hash. Adding a random salt to passwords defends against rainbow tables.

Digital Signatures provide Authentication, Integrity, and Non-repudiation. In a PKI (Public Key Infrastructure), the Certificate Authority (CA) verifies identities and issues digital certificates. Alternatively, PGP uses a decentralized Web of Trust.

  • Steganography: Hiding a secret message within the pixels of an image.
  • Homomorphic Encryption: Allows computations to be performed on ciphertext without decrypting it first.
  • Quantum Computing: Shor's algorithm can rapidly factor integers, threatening RSA and ECC.
  • IPsec Tunnel Mode: Encrypts the entire original IP packet and header for VPNs.

topics.title: Cryptanalysis and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
Cryptographic attacks attempt to deduce keys or plaintexts:

  • Known-plaintext attack: Attacker possesses samples of both plaintext and ciphertext.
  • Ciphertext-only attack: Attacker only has the encrypted messages.
  • Chosen-ciphertext attack: Attacker can choose ciphertexts and obtain their decrypted plaintexts.
  • Meet-in-the-middle attack: Exploits intermediate states, making Double DES (2DES) no more secure than single DES.
  • Birthday attack: A statistical attack aimed at finding hash collisions.
  • Protocol Vulnerabilities: POODLE exploited fallback mechanisms to force vulnerable SSL 3.0. Heartbleed was a flaw in OpenSSL allowing memory reads. TLS is the modern, secure successor to the deprecated SSL protocol.

modules.title: Advanced Cryptography and OT Deep Dive
modules.description: Integrates advanced IoT/OT operational mechanics, the Purdue model, and critical cryptographic vulnerabilities in physical devices and networks.
objectives: Understand the IoT edge layer and specialized protocols
Analyze high-impact OT/ICS threats and malware
Assess physical wireless threats to IoT devices
Perform firmware extraction and analysis

topics.title: IoT Architecture and the Edge Layer
topics.content:
In the standard IoT architecture, the Edge (Device) layer consists of the physical sensors, actuators, and smart devices that interact directly with the environment. These devices often utilize lightweight protocols like MQTT, a publish-subscribe messaging protocol designed for high-latency or unreliable networks.

Tools like Shodan operate as search engines designed to locate exposed internet-connected devices, making it trivial for attackers to find vulnerable IoT infrastructure.

topics.title: Advanced IoT and Wireless Threats
topics.content:
IoT ecosystems face unique threats:

  • Mirai Botnet: Propagated primarily by exploiting weak or factory default usernames and passwords on embedded devices.
  • RollJam Attack: A physical wireless attack against car key fobs. It jams the vehicle's receiver while simultaneously recording the rolling code sent by the fob, saving it for later unauthorized use.
  • Sybil Attack: A single malicious node spoofs multiple fake identities to gain disproportionate influence over the network, subverting routing protocols or reputation systems in P2P/IoT networks.
  • Zigbee Replay: Short-range home automation protocols like Zigbee are vulnerable to replay attacks if frame counters are not implemented correctly.

topics.title: OT/ICS Systems and Cyber-Physical Threats
topics.content:
SCADA systems monitor and control large-scale industrial processes. At the heart of these systems is the PLC (Programmable Logic Controller), a ruggedized industrial computer that directly reads sensors and operates physical machinery (valves, motors). Operators monitor these processes via a graphical dashboard called the HMI (Human-Machine Interface).

In Operational Technology (OT), Availability is the most prioritized security principle, as downtime can lead to catastrophic physical or financial consequences. The lack of security in legacy protocols like Modbus leaves these systems highly vulnerable.

A prime example of a cyber-physical attack is the Stuxnet worm, which was specifically designed to sabotage Iranian nuclear centrifuges by altering Siemens PLC logic.

topics.title: ICS Network Architecture and Firmware Analysis
topics.content:
To protect vulnerable OT components from internet and IT-based threats, organizations implement the Purdue Model (Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture). This framework divides ICS and enterprise networks into distinct hierarchical zones, enforcing strict network segmentation.

Security researchers investigating IoT/OT devices often need to analyze their embedded software. Binwalk is the industry-standard tool used for analyzing, reverse engineering, and extracting compressed file systems from binary firmware images.
